title: Safety Stock Calculator
meta_description: Calculate safety stock for a target service level. Enter the service-level factor, demand variability, and lead time to size your buffer, with the formula explained.

inputs.label: Service-level factor (z)
inputs.help: Safety factor for your target service level, e.g. 1.65 for 95% or 2.33 for 99%.

inputs.label: Std deviation of daily demand
inputs.help: Variability of daily demand. Higher variability needs more buffer.

result_cards.label: Safety stock
result_cards.description: Buffer stock to hold above expected lead-time demand.

copy_blocks.intro: Safety stock is the buffer that protects against running out when demand or supply varies. The service-level method sizes it from how much daily demand swings and how long replenishment takes.
copy_blocks.how_it_works: Pick a service-level factor (z) for your target service level, multiply by the standard deviation of daily demand, and scale by the square root of the lead time.
copy_blocks.formula_explanation: Safety stock = z x standard deviation of daily demand x sqrt(lead time). The square root reflects that variability accumulates over the lead time, not linearly.
copy_blocks.worked_example: For a 95% service level (z = 1.64), demand standard deviation of 3 units/day, and a 15-day lead time: 1.64 x 3 x sqrt(15) = about 19 units.

faq.q: What service-level factor should I use?
faq.a: Common values: 90% = 1.28, 95% = 1.65, 98% = 2.05, 99% = 2.33. Higher service levels need more buffer.

faq.q: What if lead time also varies?
faq.a: Use the combined form that adds lead-time variance: sigma over lead time = sqrt(LT x sigmaD^2 + D^2 x sigmaLT^2). This tool assumes constant lead time.

faq.q: How do I use the result?
faq.a: Feed it into the reorder point calculator so your trigger level covers both expected demand and this buffer.
